1.9: A direct revelation: the ascension is not the departure of Christ but the enthronement of the crucified-and-risen Lord. When the cloud receives Jesus (nephelē hypelaben auton), Luke invokes the Danielic cloud-of-divine-presence (Dan 7:13-14) and the Sinai theophany cloud. The Christological point: Christ is not leaving the world but entering the place of cosmic authority at the Father's right hand, from which he will send the Spirit (2:33) and to which believers' prayers are addressed. The disciples' upward gaze is redirected by the angels — not to the sky but to the world they are sent to fill with witnesses.
1.11: A direct revelation: 'This Jesus, who was taken up from you into heaven, will come in the same way as you saw him go into heaven.' The parousia-promise anchors Christian hope in the personal return of the same embodied, risen Lord who ascended — not a spiritual presence-return (already given at Pentecost) but the eschatological appearing of the glorified Christ. The continuity of identity ('this Jesus') is christologically critical: the one who comes is the one who died, who rose, who ascended — resurrection-continuity spans from the tomb to the parousia.
1.1: The prologue's 'all that Jesus began to do and teach' (ērxato ho Iēsous poiein te kai didaskein) frames Acts as the continuation of Jesus's ministry — not its memorial. The word ērxato (began) implies that Acts records what Jesus continues to do through the Spirit and the church. The Christological claim is programmatic: the risen Lord is not absent from Acts but its hidden protagonist. Every missionary journey, every miracle, every sermon is Christ acting through appointed witnesses.
1.2: The ascension marks the transition from Jesus's visible earthly ministry to his invisible governance of the church through the Spirit. 'Having given commands through the Holy Spirit to the apostles' (dia pneumatos hagiou enteilamenos) — even Jesus's earthly post-resurrection instructions were mediated by the Spirit, establishing the pattern by which his authority continues. The Christological continuity: the same Jesus who commanded during his ministry commands still, now through the Spirit's agency.
1.3: 'He presented himself alive to them after his suffering by many proofs (tekmēriois), appearing to them during forty days and speaking about the kingdom of God.' The forty days of resurrection appearances — unique to Luke-Acts — is a deliberately significant span (cf. Moses's forty days on Sinai, Israel's forty years, Jesus's forty days of testing). Christ's post-resurrection teaching about the kingdom prepares the apostles for their proclamation role. The 'many proofs' (tekmēria = conclusive evidence, the strongest Greek evidential term) grounds the resurrection in testable reality, not faith-independent-of-evidence.
1.4: 'Wait for the promise of the Father' — the Spirit is specifically Jesus's gift from the Father, the fulfillment of Joel 2:28-32 that Peter will cite at Pentecost. In John's Gospel, Jesus promises 'another Paraclete' (allos paraklētos) whom he will send (John 14:16, 15:26, 16:7). The Christological frame: Pentecost is not a spontaneous event but the deliberate fulfillment of Jesus's specific promise. The disciples are instructed to wait — the initiative is Christ's, not theirs.
1.5: 'John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit not many days from now' — the contrast between John's baptism and Spirit-baptism is fulfilled at Pentecost through Christ's gift. John himself foretold 'one who is mightier than I… he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire' (Luke 3:16). The prediction's fulfillment is explicitly Christ's action: Acts 2:33 will identify the risen, exalted Christ as the one who 'poured out this that you yourselves are seeing and hearing'. Spirit-baptism is Christ's signature act of the new age.
1.6: 'Lord, will you at this time restore the kingdom to Israel?' — the disciples' question reveals the dominant eschatological expectation: a Davidic kingdom restoring national Israel's sovereignty. Jesus's answer neither confirms nor denies but reframes the timeline as the Father's prerogative and the disciples' role as witnesses rather than administrators. The Christological point: the 'kingdom' Jesus is establishing through the Spirit's outpouring is larger than the political restoration Israel anticipated — it is the reign of God extending to the ends of the earth through witness.
1.7: 'It is not for you to know times or seasons that the Father has fixed by his own authority' — the eschatological reservation reflects the same pattern as the Gospels (Mark 13:32 — 'only the Father knows'). The Christological implication: the Son himself, in his resurrection appearances, defers to the Father's timing. The disciples are redirected from temporal calculation to missional obedience — which is not evasion of the kingdom question but its reframing. The kingdom arrives through witness, not through chronological prediction.
1.8: A theme: 'You will be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth' — the missionary mandate is simultaneously the outline of Acts itself and a Christological claim. The witnesses testify specifically to Christ — his death, resurrection, and lordship (2:32; 3:15; 5:32; 10:39-41; 13:31). The geographic expansion is not a human missionary project but Christ's own testimony spreading through witnesses he has personally appointed. Every church planted in Acts is Christ claiming his inheritance (Ps 2:8 — 'the nations as your inheritance, the ends of the earth as your possession').
1.10: The two angels' interruption of the disciples' upward gaze redirects their attention to their mission. The Christological effect: the ascension must not become the object of contemplation but the starting point of witness. Christ has gone; Christ will return; in between, witness. The disciples' posture — standing, gazing into heaven — is transformed by the promise of the return (v. 11) into active waiting and obedient witness. The Christology of the parousia frames the entire age of the church: between ascension and return, the church witnesses.
1.12: The return to Jerusalem from Olivet and the gathering in the upper room places the waiting community in the same city where Jesus was crucified and appeared risen. Jerusalem is the starting point of the Christological geography of Acts (1:8) and the place where the Spirit would be poured out. The disciples' return is an act of obedience to the risen Christ's instruction (v. 4 — 'not to depart from Jerusalem').
1.13: The eleven apostles named as gathered in the upper room are the designated witnesses Jesus had appointed. The listing — Peter, John, James, Andrew, Philip, Thomas, Bartholomew, Matthew, James son of Alphaeus, Simon the Zealot, Judas son of James — corresponds to the Twelve minus Judas Iscariot. Their presence as a named body establishes the apostolic community as the foundation of the witness-structure Christ is building. The upper room gathering continues devoted prayer while awaiting the Spirit Christ promised.
1.14: 'All these with one accord were devoting themselves to prayer, together with the women and Mary the mother of Jesus, and his brothers' — the gathered community includes those Jesus had called, the women who witnessed his resurrection (Luke 24:10), and Jesus's own family (who had not believed during his ministry, John 7:5, but are now present). The Christological transformation: the resurrection has converted even Jesus's skeptical brothers (James becomes a pillar of the Jerusalem church, 15:13; Gal 1:19). The community at prayer is itself a Christological sign — gathered by the risen Christ's promise.
1.15: Peter's standing up among the 120 brethren and taking the initiative about Judas's replacement demonstrates the resurrection's reconstituting effect on the discipleship community. Peter, who had denied Christ three times, now leads the gathered community in acting on Scripture. The Christological restoration implicit in Peter's leadership (cf. John 21:15-17 — 'feed my sheep') is now operative: the apostle of the denial is the apostle of the reconstituted Twelve.
1.16: 'The Scripture had to be fulfilled (edei plērōthēnai tēn graphēn) which the Holy Spirit spoke beforehand by the mouth of David concerning Judas.' The betrayal is not an accident but a prophetically anticipated event. The Christological implication: the crucifixion's mechanism (betrayal by an insider for silver, cf. Zech 11:12-13) was already scripted in the Psalms. Scripture's fulfillment in Judas is simultaneously a confirmation that Christ's death was not a defeat but a divine appointment. Edei (it was necessary) is the same word Jesus uses of his own suffering (Luke 24:26, 46).
1.17: Judas 'was numbered among us and was allotted his share in this ministry (tou klērou tēs diakonias tautēs)' — the vocabulary of allotment (klēros) will be used again for Matthias's election (v. 26). The divine appointment of Judas within the Twelve is christologically significant: Christ chose Judas knowing his betrayal role (John 6:64, 70-71). The election was not a mistake; the betrayal was within the divine script without removing Judas's moral responsibility.
1.18: The field bought with Judas's thirty pieces of silver echoes Zech 11:12-13 and Matt 27:3-10 (where the chief priests buy the potter's field). Acts emphasizes Judas's violent death in the field — 'falling headlong he burst open in the middle' — as the just end of apostasy and betrayal. The Christological point: the price of Christ's blood created a field of blood; the consequences of betraying the innocent circled back on Judas. The ironic justice is providential, not accidental.
1.19: 'This became known to all the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so that the field was called in their own language Akeldama, that is, Field of Blood.' The public knowledge of Judas's fate and the naming of the field memorializes the cost of betrayal. The Christological register: 'field of blood' permanently marks the consequences of the thirty silver pieces — the price assigned to the life of the Son of Man (Zech 11:12 — the insulting wage for the shepherd who was rejected by the flock).
1.20: The citation of Ps 69:25 ('Let his dwelling become desolate, let no one dwell in it') and Ps 109:8 ('Let another take his office') interprets Judas's fate and the need for replacement through the lens of the Psalms' persecuted righteous one. These are psalms of the innocent sufferer crying out against unjust persecution — psalms that the NT consistently applies to Jesus's passion (Ps 69 is cited in all four Gospels for various passion details). By applying them to Judas, Peter establishes that Judas acted against the Christ figure of the Psalms and his fate is the fate of the persecutors of the righteous.
1.21: The qualifications for apostolic office — 'one of the men who have accompanied us during all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and out among us, beginning from the baptism of John until the day when he was taken up from us' — are entirely Christocentric. An apostle is primarily a witness to the entire Christ-event: his ministry from baptism to ascension. The resurrection testimony ('one of these men must become with us a witness to his resurrection', v. 22) is the apex qualification. Apostolicity is defined by personal witness to the risen Christ.
1.22: 'One of these men must become with us a witness to his resurrection (martys tēs anastaseōs autou)' — the apostolic office is fundamentally defined as resurrection-witness. This is the Christological center of the entire apostolic enterprise: not organizational leadership, not moral exemplars, but eyewitnesses who testify 'God raised this Jesus' (2:32). The church's foundation is testimony to a specific historical event. The Matthias selection is driven entirely by this Christological requirement: who can truthfully say 'I saw the risen Christ'?
1.23: Two men are proposed: Joseph called Barsabbas and Matthias — both qualified witnesses of the Christ-event from baptism to ascension. The community prays 'You, Lord (sy Kyrie), who know the hearts of all men' — the Lord addressed is the risen Christ, the one whose choice of the Twelve was his own prerogative (Luke 6:12-16). The community defers to Christ's own election even for the replacement of one of his chosen Twelve.
1.24: 'You, Lord, who know the hearts of all, show which one of these two you have chosen' — kardiognōstēs (heart-knower) is also applied to God in 15:8. The prayer addresses the risen Lord as the one who knows the inner character of candidates — the same divine prerogative Jesus exercises in calling his disciples (John 1:42, 47-48 — knowing Simon and Nathanael before being formally introduced). The election of apostles belongs to Christ's own authority, not human discernment alone.
1.25: 'To take the place in this ministry and apostleship from which Judas turned aside to go to his own place' — the phrase 'his own place' (ton topon ton idion) is a euphemism for Judas's deserved end. The contrast with Matthias's calling to 'ministry and apostleship' frames the choice as between two destinations that were Judas's own choice and Matthias's gracious appointment. The vacancy in the Twelve was not only organizational but symbolically required filling: twelve tribes, twelve apostles — the new Israel founded on Christ must be reconstituted with the proper symbolic number.
1.26: 'They cast lots, and the lot fell on Matthias' — the casting of lots as a means of divine discernment connects to the OT Levitical practice (Lev 16:8-10; Prov 16:33 — 'the lot is cast by man but its every decision is from the LORD'). This is the last time lots are used in Acts to discern God's will; after Pentecost, the Spirit provides direct guidance. The reconstituted Twelve around the risen Christ are the symbolic foundation of the renewed Israel, the eschatological community gathered by the Christ who called and reconstituted them.

2.36: A direct revelation: 'God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified.' This is the climax of Peter's Pentecost sermon and one of the densest Christological sentences in the NT. Kurios (Lord) carries the full weight of the LXX divine name (YHWH); Christos (Christ/Messiah) the royal-covenantal designation. The resurrection-exaltation is the moment of enthronement — not that Jesus became Lord only then (his pre-existence is assumed throughout Acts' theology), but that the cosmic lordship is now publicly declared and the messianic mission fulfilled. The audience ('whom you crucified') makes the declaration forensically stunning: the crucified one is the enthroned Lord.
2.1: Pentecost is the fulfillment of Christ's specific promise ('wait for the promise of the Father', 1:4; 'you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit not many days from now', 1:5). The Spirit's arrival is not independent of Christ but is his gift from the Father (v. 33 — 'he has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing'). The first day of the new age is the day Christ pours out his Spirit, inaugurating the age of the church. Pentecost is as Christologically driven as Christmas or Easter.
2.2: The sound of a rushing wind (pnoē biaias) filling the house alludes to the Spirit of God who 'moves over the face of the waters' (Gen 1:2 — ruach), the breath of life breathed into Adam (Gen 2:7), and Ezekiel's wind bringing the dry bones to life (Ezek 37:9-14). The Christological connection: the risen Christ had breathed on his disciples and said 'Receive the Holy Spirit' (John 20:22) — Pentecost is the full outpouring of what that anticipatory act prefigured.
2.3: The fire tongues (glōssai hōsei pyros) distribute and rest on each person — not on the building or a symbol but on individuals. John had prophesied that the Coming One would 'baptize with the Holy Spirit and fire' (Luke 3:16). The fire is simultaneously the refining and consuming fire of divine holiness and the illuminating fire of divine presence. Each believer receiving the fire is a Pentecostal anointing — they become individually Christ's temples (1 Cor 6:19) just as the cloud of glory once filled Solomon's temple.
2.4: 'They were all filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues (heterais glōssais).' The Spirit-filling that drives proclamation is the first Christological act of the new age: the church begins with speech. The content of the speech (v. 11 — 'the mighty works of God') is proclamation, not ecstasy. The Spirit given by the risen Christ immediately empowers witness — the connection between Spirit-reception and bold proclamation is consistent throughout Acts (2:4; 4:8, 31; 9:17-20; 13:9-12).
2.5: The Diaspora Jews in Jerusalem for Pentecost represent the full geographic range of Israel's scattering (vv. 9-11 list fifteen regions). The Christological significance: the reversal of Diaspora begins at Pentecost. The scattered people are drawn back — not physically to Jerusalem permanently but to the Christ whom Jerusalem's proclamation will announce. The pilgrimage festival becomes the occasion for the gospel's first global-scale proclamation.
2.6: Each one heard in his own language (tē idią dialektō) — the miracle is not only speaking but hearing comprehensible proclamation. The Babel reversal (Gen 11:7-9 — language scattered so peoples could not understand one another) is undone: the Spirit of the crucified-and-risen Christ creates intelligibility across linguistic barriers. The kingdom of God speaks every human language simultaneously because Christ's lordship knows no linguistic boundary.
2.7: 'Amazed and astonished, they said, ‘Are not all these who are speaking Galileans?’' — the messengers are visibly unqualified by cultural prestige. Galilee was considered peripheral to the Jerusalem religious establishment. The Christological irony repeats: God chose what is weak and lowly to shame the strong (1 Cor 1:27). The Galilean disciples of a Galilean carpenter, filled with the Spirit of the risen Lord, become the instruments of world-spanning proclamation.
2.8: The multilingual proclamation creates a unity-in-diversity that is itself a sign of Christ's all-encompassing lordship. The rhetorical question — 'how is it that we each hear in our own native language?' — directs attention to the supernatural character of the event. No Babel-era linguistic unifier; no lingua franca; instead, simultaneous comprehension in every language. The Spirit of Christ translates the gospel without reducing it to one cultural form.
2.9: Parthians, Medes, Elamites, and residents of Mesopotamia — the eastern Diaspora, beyond the Roman Empire's eastern frontier. The list begins at the far east and works westward, geographically encompassing the entire known Jewish world. The Christological geography: Christ's first Pentecostal harvest includes Jews from every corner of the Roman world and beyond, the first down-payment of the 'ends of the earth' mission (1:8).
2.10: Phrygia and Pamphylia (Asia Minor), Egypt and Libya near Cyrene, visitors from Rome — both Jews and proselytes. The Gentile proselytes in the crowd mean that the first recipients of Pentecost's proclamation already include former Gentiles who had converted to Judaism. The Christ-event's effect on the nations begins even before the formal Gentile mission of Acts 10-11. The Spirit overflows ethnic categories from the very first day.
2.11: 'Cretans and Arabs — we hear them telling in our own tongues the mighty works of God' (tas megaleia tou theou) — the content of the Spirit-filled speech is praise of God's great acts. The phrase echoes the Exodus vocabulary (megaleia in Deut 11:2 LXX for the mighty acts of the Exodus). The Christological reframe: the mighty works of God the crowd is hearing proclaimed are not the Exodus but the resurrection — the new and greater Exodus accomplished by Christ.
2.12: 'All were amazed and perplexed, saying to one another, 'What does this mean?'' (ti an theloi touto einai) — the crowd's genuine theological question is exactly the question Peter's sermon answers. The Spirit-event creates the conditions for proclamation; the confusion becomes the opening for explanation. The crowd's perplexity is the Spirit-created invitation for the Christological announcement that follows.
2.13: The mockers' interpretation — 'they are filled with new wine' — is the persistently reductive reading that reduces divine reality to material explanation. The Christological parallel: Jesus himself was accused of being demon-possessed (Mark 3:22) and mad (John 10:20). The Spirit's coming provokes the same divided response as Jesus's ministry: amazement and faith on one side, dismissal and mockery on the other. Every generation must decide what to do with the Pentecostal evidence.
2.14: Peter stands up with the Eleven — the reconstituted apostolic community — and raises his voice in public proclamation. The Christological transformation of Peter: the man who could not admit knowing Jesus before a servant girl in a courtyard now stands before thousands in Jerusalem to proclaim the crucified-and-risen Lord. The Spirit-filling has done what three years of discipleship did not: created the boldness that resurrection-witness requires.
2.15: 'These people are not drunk as you suppose, for it is the third hour of the day' — the defense by time (9 a.m., before the first meal) is the minor point. The major claim is the Christological explanation that replaces the mocking interpretation. Peter's apologetic method is a model: engage the audience's actual charge honestly, then reframe within the scriptural narrative. The Pentecostal phenomenon demands a scriptural explanation, not a sociological one.
2.16: 'But this is what was uttered through the prophet Joel' — the pesher formula (touto estin to eirēmenon, 'this is that which was spoken') applies Joel 2:28-32 to the Pentecost event. The Christological exegesis: Peter identifies the Spirit-outpouring as the eschatological fulfillment Joel foresaw, and the 'name of the Lord' in Joel 2:32 ('everyone who calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved') as the name of Jesus (v. 21 → v. 38 — 'call on the name of the Lord' becomes 'baptized in the name of Jesus Christ').
2.17: 'Your sons and daughters will prophesy, your young men will see visions, your old men will dream dreams' — the universality of Spirit-distribution across age and gender is Christ's fulfillment of Moses's wish: 'Would that all the LORD's people were prophets, that the LORD would put his Spirit on them!' (Num 11:29). The Christological new covenant: what was exceptional in Israel (the Spirit on prophets, priests, and kings) becomes the birthright of every believer. Christ has made all his people a 'kingdom of priests' (1 Pet 2:9, citing Exod 19:6).
2.18: 'Even on my male servants and female servants I will pour out my Spirit, and they shall prophesy' — the social barriers of the old era (gender, status, age) that restricted Spirit-reception are dissolved in the Christological new age. Paul will articulate the theological foundation: 'there is no longer Jew or Greek, slave or free, male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Gal 3:28). Pentecost is the Spirit-instantiation of that Christological equality.
2.19: 'And I will show wonders in the heavens above and signs on the earth below, blood and fire and vapor of smoke' — the cosmic signs accompanying the Day of the Lord. Peter's citation suggests Pentecost inaugurates the last days; the specific cosmic signs may apply to the Jerusalem destruction of 70 CE (cf. Luke 21:11, 20-24) as the first great historical judgment within the Day of the Lord. The Christological frame: Christ's resurrection has inaugurated the eschatological age; the decisive judgment-event (the cross, then the 70 CE destruction) follows as divine verification.
2.20: 'The sun shall be turned to darkness and the moon to blood, before the day of the Lord comes, the great and magnificent day' — the Day of the Lord in OT prophecy is consistently tied to divine judgment and salvation (Amos 5:18-20; Joel 1-3; Isa 13; Zeph 1). Peter's application positions the crucifixion (the great darkness, Luke 23:44-45) within this cosmic-judgment vocabulary. The Day of the Lord has arrived in Christ's death and resurrection; all subsequent history moves toward its consummation.
2.21: 'And it shall come to pass that everyone who calls upon the name of the Lord shall be saved' — Joel's promise of universal salvation through the LORD's name is Peter's bridge to the Christological announcement that follows. In Joel's context, 'the LORD' is YHWH; in Peter's application, 'the Lord' is Jesus (v. 36 — God has made him Lord). The equation is deliberate: to call on the name of Jesus is to call on the name of the LORD. The Christological identification of Jesus with YHWH is embedded in the sermon's exegesis.
2.22: 'Jesus of Nazareth, a man attested to you by God with mighty works and wonders and signs (dynamesin kai terasi kai sēmeiois) that God did through him in your midst' — Peter establishes the historical factuality of Jesus's ministry as divine attestation. The Christological argument: if God's own power was working through Jesus's miracles (which the crowd could verify — 'as you yourselves know'), then God himself had endorsed Jesus's identity and message before the crucifixion.
2.23: 'This Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men' — the most theologically precise statement about the crucifixion in Acts. Three elements: (1) divine plan (hōrismenē boulē, the determined counsel of God); (2) human guilt ('you crucified'; 'by the hands of lawless men'); (3) foreknowledge. The cross is simultaneously the most evil act in human history and the most intentional act in divine history. Neither the divine decree cancels human guilt nor human guilt cancels divine sovereignty. The Christological center: Christ's death was neither accident nor defeat but the event God planned from before creation.
2.24: 'God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death (lysas tas ōdinas tou thanatou), because it was not possible for him to be held by it.' The resurrection is presented as divine inevitability: death could not hold the Author of life (3:15). The phrase 'pangs of death' (ōdinas) translates a Hebrew phrase sometimes rendered 'cords of Sheol' (Ps 18:4-5; 116:3) — metaphors of death's constrictive grip. Christ burst free from death's grip not because death was weak but because Christ is life itself. The resurrection is not a reversal of the crucifixion but its necessary completion.
2.25: The citation of Ps 16:8-11 ('I had the LORD always before me; because he is at my right hand, I shall not be shaken') as a resurrection psalm is Peter's most daring exegetical move. The entire Ps 16 meditation on the confident trust of the righteous one who expects God to 'not abandon my soul to Hades nor let your Holy One see corruption' is applied to Jesus's resurrection. The Christological argument: David wrote this psalm but died and his tomb remains (v. 29); the psalm therefore looks forward to David's greater son who would in fact be raised without seeing corruption.
2.26: 'Therefore my heart was glad, and my tongue rejoiced; my flesh also will dwell in hope' — the resurrection hope that Ps 16's speaker expresses is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection. The Psalmist's confident hope ('my flesh will dwell in hope') becomes the description of Christ's resurrection body — his flesh was indeed raised, not 'in hope' only but in fact. The typological argument connects the pattern of righteous suffering-and-vindication across the Psalter to its unique historical fulfillment in Jesus.
2.27: 'For you will not abandon my soul to Hades, or let your Holy One see corruption' — 'Holy One' (hosios) is a divine designation that Peter applies to Jesus (cf. 3:14 — 'the Holy and Righteous One'). The specific promise — no corruption — is the basis for the resurrection argument: the tomb was empty because God's promise to his Holy One was kept. The biological fact (no decay) is simultaneously a Christological declaration: Jesus is the Holy One who uniquely warranted and received God's resurrection promise.
2.28: 'You have made known to me the paths of life; you will make me full of gladness with your presence' — the Psalmist's hope of 'paths of life' is Christ's resurrection path out of death. The gladness 'with your presence' corresponds to the ascended Christ's exaltation at the Father's right hand (v. 33). The Christological trajectory of Ps 16 runs: faithful trust → death → resurrection → exaltation → life in God's presence. The psalm narrates the entire Christ-event from the perspective of the righteous sufferer who trusts in God's vindication.
2.29: 'Brothers, I may say to you with confidence about the patriarch David that he both died and was buried, and his tomb is with us to this day' — Peter's argument hinges on the empirical fact that David's tomb was known and occupied. The Christological exegesis requires this: if David died and his body decayed, then Ps 16's promise of 'no corruption' cannot refer to David himself. The unfulfilled promise in David's tomb drives the argument toward the one in whom the promise was kept — Jesus, whose tomb was empty.
2.30: 'Being therefore a prophet, and knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him that he would set one of his descendants on his throne' — the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16; Ps 89:3-4; Ps 132:11) provides the second prophetic line in Peter's argument. David knew, as a prophet, that the messianic promise involved a descendant who would reign forever. The Christological connection: the resurrection-exaltation of Jesus (v. 33-34) is simultaneously the fulfillment of the resurrection promise (Ps 16) and the enthronement promise (Ps 110:1) that define the Davidic covenant's ultimate scope.
2.31: 'He foresaw and spoke about the resurrection of the Christ, that he was not abandoned to Hades, and his flesh did not see corruption' — Peter's conclusion: David, the psalmist-prophet, was speaking about Christ's resurrection when he wrote Ps 16. The distinction between David's own death (v. 29) and his prophetic foresight (v. 30-31) establishes the typological hermeneutic of Acts' entire approach to the OT: the Psalms of the righteous sufferer are prophetic descriptions of Christ that exceed their Davidic context.
2.32: 'This Jesus God raised up, and of that we are all witnesses' — the proclamation formula is simple and historical. The Christological claim rests on testimony: 'we are all witnesses'. The resurrection is not a theological abstraction but an attested fact. Paul will use the same testimonial structure in 1 Cor 15:3-8 — 'he appeared to Cephas, then to the Twelve… then to more than five hundred brothers at one time'. The historicity of the resurrection is the foundation on which the entire Christological edifice stands.
2.33: 'Being therefore exalted at the right hand of God (tē dexia tou theou hypsōtheis), and having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing and hearing.' The Christological movement: crucifixion → resurrection → exaltation → Spirit-pouring. The Pentecost event that the crowd is witnessing is itself evidence of the exaltation of Christ. The visible, audible Spirit-outpouring is the exalted Lord's signature in real time — the crowd cannot argue with what they are seeing and hearing.
2.34: 'For David did not ascend into the heavens, but he himself says, 'The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand'' — Ps 110:1 applied to Christ's exaltation. As with Ps 16, Peter's argument is that David did not ascend to heaven (his tomb proves it), therefore Ps 110:1's 'my Lord' seated at YHWH's right hand is not David but David's greater son. The Christological use of Ps 110 is the most widespread OT citation in the NT (cited in Matt 22:44; Mark 12:36; Luke 20:42-43; 1 Cor 15:25; Eph 1:20; Heb 1:13, etc.) — the foundational proof-text for Christ's exaltation and lordship.
2.35: 'Until I make your enemies your footstool' — the continuing session at the Father's right hand, the ongoing subjugation of all opposition, will culminate in the parousia. The Christological eschatology: Christ currently reigns (Ps 110:1 — 'sit') while the subjugation is completed (the 'until' clause), and will return when the Father's purpose is complete. The church exists in the 'until' — between the enthronement and the footstool's completion.
2.37: 'They were cut to the heart (katenygesan tēn kardian) and said, 'Brothers, what shall we do?'' — the Spirit-driven conviction of the crowd is the Christological sermon's intended effect. The question mirrors the Baptist's hearers (Luke 3:10, 12, 14) and foreshadows the Philippian jailer (Acts 16:30). The Christological response is consistent: the crisis of recognition ('you crucified the Lord') requires a response that names Jesus as both the source of the problem and the solution to it.
2.38: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit' — the Christological formula for entry into the new community. Repentance reorients the person toward the one they crucified; baptism in Jesus's name publicly identifies them with the crucified-and-risen Lord; forgiveness is specifically through Christ's name; the Spirit is the Christological gift of the new age. The entire salvation package is Christocentrically structured.
2.39: 'The promise is for you and for your children and for all who are far off, everyone whom the Lord our God calls to himself' — the scope of salvation extends temporally (children) and spatially ('far off' — a phrase from Isa 57:19 that Paul applies to Gentiles in Eph 2:13, 17). The Christological promise made at Pentecost is universal in scope from the first day: the salvation accomplished by Christ is offered without qualification across every human boundary. The limitation is only 'everyone whom the Lord our God calls' — election, not ethnicity.
2.40: 'And with many other words he bore witness and continued to exhort them, saying, 'Save yourselves from this crooked generation'' — the call to separate from the generation that rejected Christ echoes the OT wilderness Israel's opportunity to follow or reject God (Num 14). The Christological urgency: the judgment on Jerusalem (coming in 70 CE) hangs over those who reject the Pentecostal proclamation. Salvation is not only personal (forgiveness) but historical (escape from the coming judgment on the Christ-rejecting generation).
2.41: 'Those who received his word were baptized, and there were added that day about three thousand souls' — the three thousand added at Pentecost contrasts dramatically with the three thousand who fell in the golden calf incident (Exod 32:28 — the day the law was given at Sinai). The Christological typology: where the old covenant's first day brought death, the new covenant's first day (Pentecost, the traditional date of Torah-giving) brings life. The Spirit enlivens where the letter kills (2 Cor 3:6).
2.42: 'They devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers' — the four marks of the Pentecost community are Christologically structured: (1) apostolic teaching is teaching about Christ; (2) koinōnia is fellowship in Christ's body; (3) breaking of bread is the eucharist — re-presentation of Christ's death (Luke 22:19 — 'do this in remembrance of me'); (4) prayers are in Christ's name. Every dimension of the early community's life is centered on the crucified-and-risen Lord.
2.43: 'Awe came upon every soul, and many wonders and signs were being done through the apostles' — the continuing miraculous works of the apostles are explicitly Christ's own works continued through them. Jesus had promised 'whoever believes in me will also do the works that I do; and greater works than these will he do' (John 14:12). The Christological mediation: every apostolic sign is performed 'through' them (implying Christ as the ultimate agent) and in Christ's name (3:6, 16; 4:10).
2.44: 'All who believed were together and had all things in common' (eichon apanta koina) — the communal sharing of the Jerusalem church is a spontaneous expression of new-covenant reality: if Christ has abolished the boundary between Jew and Gentile (the ultimate social barrier), the lesser barriers of property and wealth are similarly negotiable. The Christological basis for economic sharing is eschatological: those who believe the kingdom has arrived live as if the ultimate redistribution has already occurred.
2.45: 'They were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all, as any had need' — voluntary distribution according to need (not equality of outcome but responsiveness to deprivation) is the practical expression of the Christological community ethic. The pattern anticipates the Macedonian churches' generosity for the Jerusalem poor that Paul celebrates in 2 Cor 8-9 — giving as a response to Christ's own self-impoverishment ('though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor', 2 Cor 8:9).
2.46: 'Day by day, attending the temple together and breaking bread in their homes, they received their food with glad and generous hearts' — the dual practice of temple worship and house eucharist reflects the community's dual identity: still Israel, worshipping in the temple; also the new creation community gathered around Christ's table. The Christological ambiguity is temporary; by Acts 7-8 the temple relationship will be strained; by 70 CE the temple itself will be destroyed. The breaking of bread in homes is the durable, portable, post-temple form of Christ's communal presence.
2.47: 'Praising God and having favor with all the people. And the Lord added to their number day by day those who were being saved' — the growth of the community is specifically the Lord's action: ho kyrios prosetithei (the Lord was adding). The Christological sovereignty over the church's growth is consistent throughout Acts: Paul plants, Apollos waters, but God gives the growth (1 Cor 3:6). The community's attractive quality — 'favor with all the people' — is itself a Christological sign: those who live in Christ's kingdom are an appealing alternative to the world's anxious competition.

3.15: A direct revelation: 'You killed the Author of life, whom God raised from the dead.' Archēgon tēs zōēs (pioneer/author of life) is one of Acts' most original Christological titles. The paradox is maximal: the one who is the source and pioneer of life was put to death. The resurrection is not merely resuscitation but the vindication of Christ's identity — if God raised him, God endorses his claims. Peter's speech invites repentance not as moral self-improvement but as a re-orientation toward the one they killed, now established as the sole mediator of life.
3.1: The healing at the Beautiful Gate is contextually a sign-act: it demonstrates that the kingdom power Christ exercised in his ministry (healing the lame, Luke 13:11-17; 7:22) continues through the Spirit-filled apostles in his name. Peter and John's temple attendance at the hour of prayer shows they remain observant Jews; the healing will happen in the center of Israel's worship life, echoing Jesus's own signs in the temple precincts (Luke 19:45-21:37).
3.2: The man lame from birth (chōlos ek koilias mētros autou) being carried to the gate to ask alms establishes his life history: never walked, always dependent, positioned at the threshold of the holy space but excluded from full participation (Lev 21:18 excluded those with physical defects from priestly service; Qumran excluded the maimed from the eschatological community, 1QSa 2.5-7). His healing will fulfill the eschatological sign of Isa 35:6 — 'the lame man leaps like a deer'.
3.3: The lame man asks Peter and John for alms — he expects money, not healing. The Christological pattern: Jesus regularly exceeded the petitioner's request or expectation (the blind Bartimaeus asking for mercy, receiving sight; the paralytic at Bethesda expecting stirred water, receiving a healing word). The apostolic healing in Christ's name will similarly exceed what the man asked for, demonstrating that Christ's power is not bounded by human expectation or request.
3.4: 'Peter directed his gaze at him, as did John, and said, 'Look at us'' (atenisas de Petros eis auton syn tō Iōannē eipen blepson eis hēmas) — the deliberate, focused attention before the healing word echoes Jesus's healings where touch or attention preceded the act. The command 'look at us' redirects the lame man's gaze from the alms he expected. The Christological dynamic: before the name of Jesus is invoked, the man must attend to the bearers of Christ's authority.
3.5: 'He fixed his attention on them, expecting to receive something from them' — the expectation is economic, the reception will be existential. The Christological irony is consistent: people approach Jesus and his representatives seeking material benefit (bread, money, security) and encounter the infinitely greater gift of restoration to God's image. The man's economic desperation ('expecting something') will be transformed into leaping praise (v. 8) — the Christological economy always gives more than asked.
3.6: 'I have no silver and gold, but what I do have I give to you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk!' — the explicit invocation of Christ's name as the healing authority is the Christological pivot. Peter has nothing from himself; the healing authority belongs entirely to 'Jesus Christ of Nazareth' — the title preserves the historical particularity (Nazareth, the 'despised' city, John 1:46) alongside the Christological claim (Christ = Messiah). The name spoken in command is the same name that will be declared 'the only name by which we must be saved' (4:12).
3.7: 'And he took him by the right hand and raised him up, and immediately his feet and ankles were made strong' — the physical contact ('took by the right hand and raised', ēgeiren) uses the same verb as the resurrection: Jesus 'raised up' (ēgeiren) is reflected in Peter 'raising' the lame man. The Christological embodiment: the apostle's act of lifting mirrors Christ's resurrection-lifting. The healing is not merely therapeutic but eschatologically symbolic — the resurrection power of the risen Christ manifests in the healing of every broken body it touches.
3.8: 'Leaping up he stood and began to walk, and entered the temple with them, walking and leaping and praising God' — the fulfillment of Isa 35:6 ('the lame man leaps like a deer') and Isa 35:2 LXX (the lame will enter the sanctuary). The man who had never walked enters the temple — the space from which his condition had excluded him — leaping and praising God. The Christological sign: Christ's kingdom reverses the exclusions of the old order; those kept at the threshold are brought in leaping.
3.9: 'All the people saw him walking and praising God' — the public, visible character of the healing is deliberate. The apostolic signs are not private experiences but publicly verifiable events. The Christological apologetics of Acts consistently emphasize public witness: 'this has not been done in a corner' (26:26). The healed man's exuberant walking through the temple courts is the apostolic sign's own proclamation before Peter says a word.
3.10: 'They recognized him as the one who sat at the Beautiful Gate asking for alms, and they were filled with wonder and amazement at what had happened to him' — recognition is the prerequisite for the sign's apologetic force. The crowd knows this man — he was a permanent fixture at the gate. Their amazement is the Spirit-created preparation for Peter's sermon. The Christological pattern: signs create the audience; proclamation provides the interpretation. Neither is sufficient without the other.
3.11: 'While he clung to Peter and John, all the people, utterly astounded, ran together to them in Solomon's Portico' — Solomon's Portico (the eastern colonnade of the temple courts) was a known gathering point (John 10:23 — Jesus walked there; Acts 5:12 — the apostles gathered there). The Christological use of the temple space: Jesus himself taught in Solomon's Portico; now his apostles, in his name, draw the same crowds. The continuity between Jesus's Jerusalem ministry and the apostolic ministry is geographic as well as theological.
3.12: Peter immediately redirects attention: 'Men of Israel, why do you wonder at this, or why do you stare at us, as though by our own power or piety we have made him walk?' — the Christological humility is strategic. The temptation in miraculous signs is to divert attention to the instrument. Peter's deflection is the pattern of all authentic Christological ministry: the apostle points away from himself to Christ. The healing was 'by faith in his name' (v. 16) — Christ remains the agent even when apostles are the instruments.
3.13: 'The God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, the God of our fathers, glorified his servant Jesus' — Peter's Christological proclamation in the temple begins by embedding Jesus within Israel's covenant history. The fourfold patriarchal identification establishes that the God who glorified Jesus is Israel's covenant God, not a foreign deity. The 'servant' (pais) identification connects Jesus to the Isaianic Servant (Isa 42:1; 52:13; 53:11) — the one whom the God of Israel appointed for the salvation of Israel and the nations.
3.14: 'But you denied the Holy and Righteous One (ton hagion kai dikaion), and asked for a murderer to be granted to you' — the titles 'Holy One' and 'Righteous One' are the Christological antitheses of what the crowd chose: they preferred Barabbas (a murderer) over the Holy and Righteous One. The double title carries OT weight: 'Holy One' is a divine name (Isa 6:3; 43:3); 'Righteous One' (dikaios) is the Servant's designation (Isa 53:11). Peter is declaring that the one they rejected bore the titles that defined the object of Israel's hope.
3.16: 'And his name — by faith in his name — has made this man strong whom you see and know, and the faith that is through Jesus has given the man this perfect health in the presence of you all.' The healing is explicitly mediated through 'his name' and 'faith in his name' and 'faith through Jesus' — three iterations of the same Christological point. The name of the risen Jesus is not a magical formula but the invocation of the living person whose authority over creation persists through resurrection. The 'perfect health' (holoklērian, complete wholeness) is the total restoration that the name of Jesus accomplishes.
3.17: 'Now, brothers, I know that you acted in ignorance, as did also your rulers' — the Christological mercy: Peter offers his audience the same amnesty that Jesus extended from the cross ('Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do', Luke 23:34). The category of ignorance-sin carries the OT provision for inadvertent transgression (Lev 5; Num 15:27-31). The crucifixion was the greatest crime in history; the offer of repentance and forgiveness for it is the greatest mercy in history — only possible because Christ bore the consequence.
3.18: 'But what God foretold by the mouth of all the prophets, that his Christ would suffer, he thus fulfilled' — the 'necessary suffering' of the Christ is the interpretive key to the OT. 'All the prophets' is comprehensive: not selected proof-texts but the entire prophetic witness to the Messiah's fate. Peter's claim is that a suffering Christ is not a Christian invention but the consistent message of Israel's prophets — the scandal is that Israel's own Scripture predicted what Israel could not accept. The Christological hermeneutic of Acts reads all prophecy toward the suffering-and-risen Messiah.
3.19: 'Repent therefore, and turn back (epistrepsate), that your sins may be blotted out' — the invitation to repentance is structured as a return: epistrephō (turn back/return) is the LXX verb for Israel's return to YHWH in the prophets (Mal 3:7; Isa 44:22; Jer 3:7). The Christological content of repentance: turning toward Christ is the new form of turning toward YHWH, because God has now defined himself definitively in Christ. The blotting out of sins (exaleiphthēnai, completely erased) is the new covenant fulfillment of Isa 43:25 and 44:22.
3.20: 'That times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord' — the eschatological promise tied to repentance is 'times of refreshing' (kairoi anapsyxeōs, seasons of rest/breathing space). The Christological hope: when Israel repents and turns to Christ, the fullness of eschatological blessing follows. The sending of 'the Christ appointed for you, Jesus' (v. 20b) points to the parousia as the telos of Israel's repentance. The mission to Israel is not merely evangelistic but eschatologically freighted.
3.21: 'Whom heaven must receive until the time for restoring all the things about which God spoke by the mouth of his holy prophets long ago' — Christ's present session in heaven ('heaven must receive him') is the condition of the 'universal restoration' (apokatastaseōs pantōn) still outstanding. The Christological eschatology of Acts: Christ reigns now but the full restoration awaits his return. Peter's preaching in the temple envisions Israel's repentance as the precondition for the parousia, consistent with Paul's theology in Romans 11:25-26.
3.22: 'Moses said, 'The Lord God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers. You shall listen to him in whatever he tells you'' — the Deuteronomy 18:15-18 promise of the Prophet-like-Moses is Peter's OT climax for the sermon. The Christological typology: Jesus recapitulates the Moses pattern (deliverer, mediator, lawgiver, miracle-worker) but at a cosmic scale. The command 'listen to him' echoes the Transfiguration's heavenly voice (Luke 9:35 — 'This is my Son, my Chosen One; listen to him!'). Moses points to and demands attention to his greater successor.
3.23: 'And it shall be that every soul who does not listen to that prophet shall be destroyed from the people' — the urgency of the Christological claim: failure to heed the Prophet-like-Moses carries covenant consequences. The warning is not new (Deut 18:19 — God himself will require it); Peter applies it to rejection of Jesus. The Christological exclusivity is not arbitrary: Moses himself foresaw that the ultimate Prophet would require ultimate allegiance. To reject Jesus is to reject the one Moses pointed toward and warned against rejecting.
3.24: 'And all the prophets who have spoken, from Samuel and those who came after him, also proclaimed these days' — Peter's claim is that all prophetic history, from Samuel onward, pointed to 'these days' — the days of Christ's coming, suffering, resurrection, and the Spirit's outpouring. The Christological reading of the whole prophetic canon is programmatic for Acts: the prophets were not merely recording history but previewing the Christ-event. The arrival of 'these days' is the fulfillment that all prophecy was building toward.
3.25: 'You are the sons of the prophets and of the covenant that God made with your fathers, saying to Abraham, 'And in your offspring shall all the families of the earth be blessed'' — the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3; 22:18) cited as the broadest frame for the gospel's universal scope. Paul cites this same text in Gal 3:8 and identifies 'your offspring' as Christ (Gal 3:16 — singular sperma). The Christological seed of Abraham through whom all nations are blessed is Jesus; the blessing going to 'all the families of the earth' is the Gentile mission that Acts will narrate.
3.26: 'God, having raised up his servant, sent him to you first, to bless you by turning every one of you from your wickedness' — the resurrection-sending connects directly to the Abrahamic promise: Christ raised is Christ sent. The 'first' priority for the Jewish audience is mission strategy (Rom 1:16 — 'to the Jew first and also to the Greek'), not ethnic privilege. The blessing Christ brings — 'turning every one of you from your wickedness' — is the Christological fulfillment of the covenant promise: in the seed of Abraham, wickedness is turned around, the blessing flows. The sermon closes where the gospel's trajectory begins: with Christ as the Servant-Messiah sent by Israel's God to bless the world.

4.12: A direct revelation: 'There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among people by which we must be saved.' The exclusivity claim (ouk estin en allō oudeni hē sōtēria) is made before the supreme Jewish court that had condemned Jesus. Peter's Christocentrism is absolute and public: not 'Jesus is one path among many' but the singular name by which salvation comes. The dei (must) is divine necessity — salvation through this name is not a human option but a divine appointment. The Christological uniqueness of Acts 4:12 is the evangelical confession that animates the entire mission of the book.
4.1: The Sadducees' opposition to the apostles is rooted in their denial of resurrection — but the resurrection of Jesus is precisely the claim Peter has just made (3:15, 26). Jesus had predicted this opposition: 'they will hand you over to the local councils' (Matt 10:17); 'when they bring you before synagogues and rulers and authorities, do not be anxious about how you should defend yourself' (Luke 12:11). The arrest of the apostles for proclaiming resurrection in Jesus is the first fulfillment of the passion narrative's pattern extending into the community.
4.2: The apostles are teaching 'the resurrection of the dead in Jesus' (tēn anastasin tēn ek nekrōn en tō Iēsou) — not resurrection as a general doctrine but specifically through Jesus as the firstfruit case. This is the precise christological claim that enrages the Sadducees and defines the early mission: resurrection has happened, concretely, in a named person, and this person is the basis for universal resurrection hope. The pattern of religious authority opposition to resurrection proclamation fulfills Jesus's warnings in Luke 21:12–15.
4.3: The detention 'until the next day' because it was already evening reflects the same Sanhedrin procedural constraints that governed Jesus's trial — formal Jewish trials could not be conducted at night (m. Sanhedrin 4:1). The pattern of arrest → overnight detention → trial the next morning mirrors the passion narrative's structure. Peter and John stand where Jesus stood.
4.4: Five thousand men believe despite the arrest — the multiplication of the community under opposition is the fulfillment of Jesus's parable of the sower (Luke 8:8, 'a hundredfold') and his prediction that 'the gates of hell shall not prevail' against the church (Matt 16:18). Opposition from the authorities accelerates rather than stops the mission's growth — a pattern that recurs throughout Acts and that Jesus had explicitly predicted ('they will persecute you from city to city,' Matt 23:34).
4.5: The gathering of 'rulers, elders, and scribes' in Jerusalem is the same body that condemned Jesus — Annas, Caiaphas, and the high priestly family appear by name in v. 6. The apostles are arraigned before the identical tribunal. Luke's christological point is structural: what the Sanhedrin did to Jesus it is now doing to Jesus's witnesses, and the outcome will mirror the pattern — apparent defeat by institutional power, followed by divine vindication.
4.6: Annas (still treated as the real high priest despite formal deposition by Rome) and Caiaphas (his son-in-law, current high priest) are the same pair who presided over Jesus's trial (Luke 22:54; John 18:13, 24). Their reappearance creates explicit continuity between the trial of Jesus and the trial of his witnesses. The same hands that handed Jesus to Pilate now interrogate the witnesses to his resurrection.
4.7: 'By what power or by what name did you do this?' — the Sanhedrin's question about authority echoes their challenge to Jesus in Luke 20:2 ('By what authority do you do these things?'). Jesus had answered with a counter-question and a veiled claim to divine authority; Peter will answer directly with a name — the name they condemned. The reversal is total: what they meant to suppress by crucifixion has become the operative power in Jerusalem.
4.8: 'Filled with the Holy Spirit' — Peter's Spirit-empowered defense fulfills Jesus's promise exactly: 'When they bring you before synagogues and rulers and authorities, do not be anxious about how you should defend yourself or what you should say, for the Holy Spirit will teach you in that very hour what you ought to say' (Luke 12:11–12; cf. Matt 10:19–20). The fulfillment is precise: the moment of Sanhedrin interrogation triggers the promised Spirit-speech.
4.9: 'If we are being examined today concerning a good deed done to a crippled man' — Peter frames the interrogation as absurdity: the authorities are investigating a healing. The framing is rhetorically powerful and theologically pointed: the Sanhedrin which condemned the one who healed on the Sabbath (Luke 6:6–11; 13:10–17) is now interrogating those who healed in his name. The pattern of authorities opposing the good that Jesus and his agents do is the fulfillment of Jesus's indictment: 'Woe to you lawyers, for you have taken away the key of knowledge; you did not enter yourselves, and you hindered those who were entering' (Luke 11:52).
4.10: 'By the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth whom you crucified, whom God raised from the dead — by him this man is standing before you well.' The direct naming of the Sanhedrin's crime — 'whom you crucified' — echoes Peter's Pentecost sermon (2:23, 36) and is the christological center of the defense. The contrast 'you crucified / God raised' is the gospel's essential polarity: human rejection and divine vindication. The healed man standing is the visible evidence of resurrection power operative in Jesus's name.
4.11: 'This Jesus is the stone that was rejected by you, the builders, which has become the cornerstone.' Peter cites Psalm 118:22 — the same text Jesus cited in the temple (Luke 20:17: the stone the builders rejected becoming the cornerstone). Jesus had applied it to himself and to his rejection by the chief priests; Peter now applies it directly to the crucifixion and resurrection, with the Sanhedrin as the builders and Jesus as the rejected-then-vindicated stone. The christological argument is complete: the scripture predicted this rejection, the rejection has occurred, and the resurrection confirms the scripture's fulfillment.
4.13: 'Unlettered and ordinary men' (agrammatos kai idiōtai) — the Sanhedrin's assessment that Peter and John had no formal scribal training (no studied at the feet of a recognized rabbi) makes their eloquence inexplicable without the Spirit. 'They recognized that they had been with Jesus' — the perceived connection is not formal discipleship but the distinctive presence of one who walked with Jesus. The fulfilled word: 'A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone when he is fully trained will be like his teacher' (Luke 6:40). Being with Jesus has transferred something to the disciples that scribal training alone cannot explain.
4.14: The healed man standing with Peter and John renders the Sanhedrin's situation impossible — they cannot deny the sign without looking absurd before the city. The man's presence as a walking proof defeats the institutional power's ability to suppress the truth. Jesus had said, 'If these were silent, the very stones would cry out' (Luke 19:40) — and here, in a sense, a man who was formerly immobile is the irrefutable witness.
4.15: The Sanhedrin's private deliberation mirrors their private deliberations about Jesus (Luke 22:66–71; Mark 14:55–64) — the judicial body again trying to manage a situation they cannot control. Their problem now is not Jesus (whom they killed) but the impossible persistence of his effects: a healed man, Spirit-filled disciples, and a growing community. The resurrection has made their solution to the Jesus problem worse than the problem itself.
4.16: 'We cannot deny it' — the Sanhedrin's internal confession of impotence is Luke's ironic climax. The ruling body of Israel acknowledges, in private, that they cannot suppress the evidence of Jesus's power. The institution that condemned Jesus as a false messiah who could not save himself now confesses it cannot stop what his name produces. The public suppression they choose (v. 17) in the face of private acknowledgment is the continuation of the bad faith that characterized the Passion.
4.17: 'That it may spread no further' — the Sanhedrin's agenda is containment: the same word (diaphtheirō, diaskorpizō) Jesus used for spreading. Their command mirrors Caiaphas's pragmatic judgment at Jesus's trial: 'It is better that one man die for the people' (John 11:49–50) — institutional management of theological danger through suppression. But Jesus had predicted the outcome: 'What you have whispered in private rooms shall be proclaimed on the housetops' (Luke 12:3).
4.18: 'Not to speak or teach at all in the name of Jesus' — the explicit prohibition is the empire of human authority attempting to silence the name. Jesus had said, 'Everyone who acknowledges me before men, I also will acknowledge before my Father' (Matt 10:32); the apostles' refusal to comply with this prohibition is the lived enactment of this acknowledgment. The prohibition creates the conditions for the confession of vv. 19–20.
4.19: 'Whether it is right in God's sight to listen to you rather than to God, you must judge' — Peter and John's response is the foundational statement of Christian civil disobedience: where human authority conflicts with divine command, divine authority supersedes. The formulation echoes Socrates' defense in the Apology (Plato, Apol. 29d: 'I shall obey God rather than you') — a point Luke's educated audience would recognize. But the christological grounding is different: Peter and John obey God because God has raised Jesus and commanded proclamation in his name.
4.20: 'We cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard' — eyewitness obligation as the grounds for disobedience. The 'we have seen and heard' is the epistemic foundation of apostolic witness (cf. 1 John 1:1–3; 2 Pet 1:16). Jesus had said, 'You are witnesses of these things' (Luke 24:48) — the witness commission overrides the Sanhedrin's suppression command because it comes from a higher authority.
4.21: The release without punishment — because the city glorifies God for the healing — mirrors the partial protection Jesus enjoyed from the crowds during his Jerusalem ministry (Luke 19:47–48; 22:2). The people function as a check on the Sanhedrin's power, preventing official violence where the evidence of divine action is too public to suppress. The risen Lord's community operates under the same dynamic that protected Jesus himself: popular recognition of divine power constrains official hostility.
4.22: The man was over forty years old — a detail that emphasizes the duration and certainty of his disability (congenital lame from birth, Acts 3:2, now over forty). No natural explanation could account for recovery from this condition. The age detail is Luke the physician's marker: this is medically impossible, which makes the theological claim unavoidable.
4.23: The apostles' return to 'their own people' — the community of disciples — and their report of the Sanhedrin's words triggers a corporate prayer that is one of the most christologically dense passages in Acts. The community's response to institutional opposition is not political maneuvering but prayer grounded in scripture, specifically Psalm 2. The gathering mirrors Jesus's own post-temptation return to prayer and community.
4.24: 'Sovereign Lord, who made the heaven and the earth and the sea and everything in them' — the prayer opens with God as creator and sovereign (despota), a title that frames the Sanhedrin's authority as derivative and limited. The God who made everything is not threatened by the rulers of a small province; the prayer's opening establishes the perspective from which all that follows is interpreted. The community prays from the vantage point of divine sovereignty, not human anxiety.
4.25: 'Who through the mouth of our father David, your servant, said by the Holy Spirit: Why did the Gentiles rage, and the peoples plot in vain?' — Psalm 2:1–2 is cited as directly prophetic of the current moment. The attribution — through David, by the Holy Spirit — makes David the instrument and the Spirit the author, consistent with early Christian pneumatology of scripture (cf. 2 Pet 1:21). The 'nations raging' against the Lord's anointed is being fulfilled now, in Jerusalem, against Jesus and his witnesses.
4.26: 'The kings of the earth set themselves, and the rulers were gathered together, against the Lord and against his Anointed.' The prayer identifies Herod and Pilate as the 'kings and rulers' of Psalm 2 — Gentile and Jewish power collaborating against Jesus, God's Anointed (christos). The interpretive move is bold: a psalm about universal Gentile opposition to God's king is applied to the specific historical alliance of Rome and Jerusalem against Jesus. The cosmic frame makes Pilate and Herod actors in a drama written before their birth.
4.27: 'Herod and Pontius Pilate, along with the Gentiles and the peoples of Israel' — the specific historical agents of Jesus's crucifixion are named as the fulfillment of Psalm 2. The four-fold listing (Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, Israel) is comprehensive: all human power structures participated. This is not anti-Semitic targeting of Israel alone but a universal indictment — every human power structure aligned against God's anointed. The prayer names it clearly to frame the present opposition (Sanhedrin) as a continuation of the same alliance.
4.28: 'To do whatever your hand and your plan had predestined to take place' — the crucifixion was not a defeat but a divine plan fulfilled. God's prothesis (foreordained purpose) governed the actions of even those who opposed it: the same omnipotent sovereignty that created heaven and earth (v. 24) also ordained that the Anointed's rejection would accomplish the plan of redemption. The community's confidence in the face of opposition is grounded in this recognition: opposition itself is within God's providential scope.
4.29: 'And now, Lord, look upon their threats and grant to your servants to continue to speak your word with all boldness' — the prayer does not ask for removal of opposition but for courage within it. Jesus had prayed for his disciples in the same posture: 'I do not ask that you take them out of the world, but that you keep them from the evil one' (John 17:15). The request for parrēsia (boldness/freedom of speech) is specifically the Spirit-given courage Jesus promised for Sanhedrin appearances (Luke 12:11–12).
4.30: 'While you stretch out your hand to heal, and signs and wonders are performed through the name of your holy servant Jesus' — the prayer requests continued healing signs in Jesus's name. 'Holy servant' (ton hagion paida sou) echoes the Isaiah 42–53 Servant songs; the same title was used in 3:13, 26. The community prays for the continuation of the healing ministry Jesus modeled through his earthly life (Luke 4:18–19) now mediated through his risen name.
4.31: 'The place was shaken, and they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and continued to speak the word of God with boldness.' The immediate Spirit-filling and shaking of the place is a mini-Pentecost — the Spirit fulfills Jesus's promise in real time in response to prayer. The pattern: prayer → Spirit-filling → boldness → proclamation mirrors Acts 2 (prayer, 1:14 → Spirit, 2:4 → bold proclamation, 2:14). The Spirit does not come once but repeatedly as the community returns to prayer.
4.32: 'The full number of those who believed were of one heart and soul, and no one said that any of the things that belonged to him was his own, but they had everything in common.' The description of the early community's unity and sharing echoes the idealized vision of Israel's covenant community — the Deuteronomic hope that there be 'no poor among you' (Deut 15:4). Jesus had called his disciples away from possessiveness: 'sell your possessions and give to the needy' (Luke 12:33); 'any one of you who does not renounce all that he has cannot be my disciple' (Luke 14:33). The community is embodying the Sermon's ethics corporately.
4.33: 'With great power the apostles were giving their testimony to the resurrection of the Lord Jesus, and great grace was upon them all.' The resurrection testimony and the common life are inseparably linked: the community's generosity is the visible evidence of a people who believe death has been defeated. If Jesus is risen, possessions have a different status; if death is conquered, the hoarding impulse — fundamentally a death-anxiety response — is transformed. The 'great grace' (charis megalē) on the community is the overflow of the grace that raised Jesus.
4.34: 'There was not a needy person among them' — the fulfillment of Deuteronomy 15:4 LXX: 'there shall be no poor person among you.' The Deuteronomic promise was conditional on Israel's full obedience to the covenant; the early Christian community, constituted around the risen Lord and filled with the Spirit, fulfills it organically rather than by legal compliance. Jesus had promised: 'seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you' (Matt 6:33); the community's provision for the poor is the kingdom's concrete manifestation.
4.35: 'Distribution was made to each as any had need' — the principle of distribution according to need rather than status or merit reflects the kingdom's inversions. Jesus's feeding miracles (Luke 9:12–17) had demonstrated this principle physically: everyone receives what they need. The apostles' mediating role in distribution mirrors Jesus's role in the feeding miracles where the disciples serve as intermediaries of divine provision.
4.36: Joseph Barnabas — 'son of encouragement' (huios paraklēseōs) — is introduced precisely because his generosity will contrast with the Ananias-Sapphira deception in the next chapter. The naming of a specific generous giver before the narrative of false generosity serves to distinguish genuine from counterfeit covenant fidelity. Barnabas is a Levite of Cypriot origin — unexpected generosity from an unexpected quarter, consistent with Jesus's reversals of expectation about who embodies covenant faithfulness.
4.37: Barnabas sells a field and brings the full proceeds. His act establishes the communal norm that Ananias will violate — the contrast is deliberate. The unreserved giving of Barnabas reflects the spirit of Jesus's call: 'sell all that you have and distribute to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven, and come, follow me' (Luke 18:22). Whether the early community expected all property to be sold is debated, but Barnabas models the total-surrender posture Jesus modeled.

7.56: A direct revelation: 'Behold, I see the heavens opened, and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God.' Stephen's vision at the moment of martyrdom is the only post-resurrection appearance of the risen Christ to someone other than Paul in Acts (the Damascus road, 9:4-5). The 'Son of Man' title — Jesus's own self-designation during his ministry (Luke 9:22; 22:69) — is used here by Stephen, the only NT speaker outside the Gospels to use this title of Jesus. The standing Christ (see mkt-original note on hestōta) is the risen Lord actively receiving his first martyr. Christology is here embedded in pneumatology: the Spirit-filled Stephen sees the Christological reality that sustains martyrdom.
7.1: The high priest's question — 'Are these things so?' — opens the judicial hearing that becomes Stephen's great Christological sermon. The format (defendant given opportunity to speak before the Sanhedrin) mirrors Jesus's own trial (Luke 22:66-71). Stephen's response will not be a defense of himself but an indictment of the court — the pattern of the prophets who spoke truth before power, the pattern Christ himself modeled (Luke 23:2-3, 8-9).
7.2: 'The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham' (ho theos tēs doxēs ōphthē tō patri hēmōn Abraam) — Stephen's opening is a Christological thesis in miniature. The God who reveals himself as 'glory' (the Shekinah tradition) appeared to Abraham outside the land and before the covenant institutions. This establishes the pattern Stephen will trace: divine initiative precedes and exceeds every institutional form. The 'God of glory' will appear again at the end of the speech — in the face of the crucified Jesus, whom Stephen sees enthroned (v. 55-56).
7.3: God's call to Abraham — 'Go out from your land and from your kindred' (Gen 12:1) — is the paradigmatic form of the divine call that requires leaving the familiar to follow God into the unknown. The Christological echo: Jesus himself calls disciples to leave family and possessions (Luke 14:26-27); the cross is the ultimate 'going out from' the security of this age. Abraham's obedient departure without knowing where he was going (Heb 11:8) is the type of faith the crucified-and-risen Christ calls his followers to.
7.4: Abraham's journey from Ur to Haran to Canaan — a life of pilgrimage without permanent settlement — prefigures the Christological community that 'has no lasting city but seeks the city to come' (Heb 13:14). The Christological reading of the patriarchal narrative: the people of God are constitutively a people in movement, oriented toward promise rather than possession. Jesus himself 'had nowhere to lay his head' (Luke 9:58) — the pattern of homeless pilgrimage is the Christological mode of existence.
7.5: 'Yet he gave him no inheritance in it, not even a foot's length, but promised to give it to him as a possession and to his offspring after him, though he had no child' — the promise without present possession is the structure of Abrahamic faith. The Christological fulfillment: the inheritance promised to Abraham's offspring is ultimately secured through Christ, the singular seed (Gal 3:16). Abraham believed a resurrection-like promise — a dead womb brought to life — pointing toward the resurrection of the one in whom all nations are blessed.
7.6: 'His offspring would be sojourners in a land belonging to others, enslaved and afflicted for four hundred years' — the Egypt prophecy (Gen 15:13) establishes the pattern: God's people suffer in foreign servitude before the great deliverance. The Christological typology: Christ's own suffering and death is the supreme instance of the righteous one's affliction — and his resurrection is the supreme Exodus. The Exodus story that Stephen is tracing is the type whose antitype is the cross and resurrection.
7.7: 'But I will judge the nation that they serve, and after that they shall come out and worship me in this place' — the Exodus promise includes both divine judgment on oppressors and the gathering of God's people for worship. The Christological fulfillment: the judgment on the powers that crucified Christ is executed in the resurrection (which vindicates Christ and condemns the accusers); the gathering for worship is the apostolic community of Acts. 'This place' is ultimately not a geographic location but the presence of the risen Lord.
7.8: 'And he gave him the covenant of circumcision' — the sign-covenant given to Abraham and transmitted through Isaac, Jacob, and the twelve patriarchs establishes the covenant people's identity. The Christological frame: Paul will argue (Rom 4; Gal 3) that circumcision was a sign of the righteousness of faith that Abraham had before circumcision — the covenant of promise precedes and exceeds the covenant of signs. Christ fulfills the promise, not merely the sign.
7.9: 'And the patriarchs, jealous of Joseph, sold him into Egypt; but God was with him' (ho de theos ēn met' autou) — the Joseph narrative introduces the defining pattern of Stephen's Christological typology: the rejected deliverer whom God vindicates. Joseph is sold by his brothers (type of Christ betrayed by one of his own), suffers in Egypt (type of Christ's humiliation), but God's presence persists through the rejection and through it accomplishes the salvation of those who rejected him.
7.10: 'And rescued him out of all his afflictions and gave him favor and wisdom before Pharaoh, king of Egypt, who made him ruler over Egypt and over all his household' — God's vindication of Joseph through elevation to Pharaoh's right hand is the Joseph-type's closest correspondence to the resurrection-exaltation of Christ. The rejected one becomes the sovereign; the one whom brothers wanted dead becomes the one through whom they live. Christ seated at the right hand of God (2:33-34) completes what Joseph's story prefigured.
7.11: The famine that drove Jacob's sons to Egypt — forcing the very men who sold Joseph to come to him for grain — enacts the reversal pattern of Christological typology. The rejected one becomes the only source of life; those who rejected him must come to him or die. The gospel's logic is structurally identical: the one rejected and crucified is now the only source of life for those who crucified him (3:14-15 — 'you killed the Author of life, whom God raised').
7.12: 'Jacob heard that there was grain in Egypt, and he sent our fathers out on their first visit' — the first visit without recognition parallels the pattern of rejection-then-recognition that runs through both the Joseph story and Luke's Gospel (the disciples on the Emmaus road who did not recognize the risen Christ until the breaking of bread, Luke 24:13-35). Divine provision is present before recognition dawns.
7.13: 'On the second visit Joseph made himself known to his brothers' — the second-visit revelation (Gen 45:1-15) is the moment of recognition and reconciliation. The Christological echo: Christ's second appearing — the parousia — will be the universal moment of recognition, when 'every eye will see him, even those who pierced him' (Rev 1:7; cf. Zech 12:10). The Joseph narrative's two-visit structure prefigures the two-advent pattern of Christological revelation.
7.14: Joseph sent for Jacob and all his family — seventy-five souls (LXX count) — to come to Egypt. The gathering of the whole family around the vindicated Joseph corresponds to the gathering of Israel around the vindicated Christ. The Christological community is assembled by the exalted one who sends his Spirit to draw his people to himself (John 12:32 — 'when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself').
7.15: Jacob's death in Egypt and the burial promise — both Jacob and Joseph died there, though the promise of the land remained unfulfilled — maintains the tension of Christological promise-without-possession that Stephen is tracing. The patriarchs lived and died as sojourners whose hope reached beyond their lifetimes. The Christological community lives in the same structure: the promise is certain but its fullness awaits the parousia.
7.16: The burial of the patriarchs at Shechem (in the tomb Abraham bought from the sons of Hamor) — Stephen's account differs from the Genesis record (which places Jacob at Machpelah) and may reflect alternative traditions or deliberate summary.
